content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 14, 2015

Thomas T. Watson
City Attorney
1901 Airport Road, Suite 300
South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-222

Dear Mr. Watson:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City Councilmember Tom Davis
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section
1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other areas of law, including the Public Contract
Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete
and accurate.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we forwarded your request and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the El Dorado County District
Attorney's Office pursuant to Section 1097.1(c)(3). We did not receive a written response from
either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We further advise you that for purposes of Section 1090 the
following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the
requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does either Section 1090 or the Act limit Councilmember Tom Davis's participation in a
city council vote to award a contract for a socio-economic study of the impact of vacation home
rental properties ("VHR") in the South Lake Tahoe area?

CONCLUSION

No. Given the current facts, neither law limits his participation on this particular issue.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-222
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a City Attorney for South Lake Tahoe and you write on behalf of Councilmember
Tom Davis. In 2003, the city enacted the Vacation Home Rental Program to deal with the rise in
vacation homes rented (VHR) to tourists by property owners on a short-term basis. The number of
permitted VHRs is currently 1800 properties. The city council has requested a Socio-Economic
Impact Study of Vacation Home Rentals ("Study") to take a comprehensive look at the magnitude of
VHRs and help identify the positive and adverse impacts on the community's social and economic
well-being. The city's Request for Proposal (RFP) seeks to contract with a consultant to prepare the
Study "using an academic and holistic approach." The "comprehensive, unbiased report" is to
assess how "VHRs impact the city, community, housing market and day-to-day quality of life for
residents." The city's RFP states the following objective of conducting this study:

The objective of conducting this study is to measure the potential impacts of Vacation
Home Rentals on current and future residents. The indicators should include, but not
be limited to, the following:*

☐ Changes in community demographics;

☐ Changes to character of residential neighborhoods;

☐ Changes to quality of life;

☐ Changes in employment and income levels;

☐ Lodging inventory supply and demand;

☐ Housing market analysis;

☐ Demand for public services; and

☐ Total economic impact of VHR industry to the City.

*Consultant has complete discretion to include other objectives they deem
beneficial for the City to consider to help better understand the socio-economic
impacts of Vacation Home Rentals

You state that the Study will be used to determine policy direction regarding regulation of the VHR
industry.

Councilmember Tom Davis owns a VHR property management company which presumably
includes properties in the city. He currently does not vote on any city council item regarding the
regulation of the city's VHR industry. The Councilmember wishes to vote on the award of the
VHR Study contract.

File No. A-15-222
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest under Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Sigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.)

We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether Councilmember Davis will have a conflict of interest under Section 1090 if he votes to award the Study contract.

Steps One, Two, and Three: Is Councilmember Davis subject to Section 1090 and is there a contract at issue in which the city council will participate?

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are members." City councils and their members are plainly covered by this prohibition. (See, e.g., Thomson, supra, at p. 645; City Council v. McKinley (1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 213; and see Carvallo Advice Letter, A-15-140.) We have previously found that a request for proposal is a contract under Section 1090 because the public entity will contract with the winning bidder. (See Kies Advice Letter, A-14-101.)

This matter involves a councilmember, an RFP, and the award of the Study contract by the city council.

Step Four: Does Councilmember Davis have a financial interest in the contract?

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions indicate that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) The phrase "financially interested" as used in Section 1090 means any financial

File No. A-15-222
Page No. 4

interest that might interfere with a city officer's unqualified devotion to his public duty. (People v. Watson (1971) 15 Cal.App.3d 28, 37-38.)

More recently, in Eden Township Healthcare Dist. v. Sutter Health (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 208, the court held that "to be prohibited under section 1090, the public official's financial interest must be related to the contract . . . The purpose of the prohibition is to prevent a situation where a public official would stand to gain or lose something with respect to the making of a contract over which in his official capacity he could exercise some influence." (Id. at p. 225)) Importantly, the court held that "if the contract itself offers no benefit to the official, either directly or indirectly, then the official is not financially interested in the contract . . . ." (Id. at 228.)

In this matter, the city will enter into a contract with a consultant to perform a study of VHR's impacts on the city. The main issue is whether the award of the Study contract will directly or indirectly cause Councilmember Davis to possibly profit or suffer losses. You have not provided any facts that would indicate Councilmember Davis would possibly profit or suffer losses in his VHR business, related to the award of the Study contract. It appears that Councilmember Davis will continue to recuse himself from city council policy decisions which may result from the findings of the Study. Accordingly, based on the facts you have provided, Councilmember Davis does not have a financial interest in the Study contract, may participate in the decision to award the contract, and the city council will not violate Section 1090 by entering into the contract described. No further analysis is required under Section 1090.

Conflict of Interest under the Act

Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the official and is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, emphasis added).

Financial Interests

Financial interests include:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth at least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).)

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public official within 12 months before the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

File No. A-15-222
Page No. 5

• Any donor of gift(s) amounting to a total of at least $460 within 12 months before a decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

• A public official's own personal finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103(.)

As the owner of a VHR management company, Councilmember Davis has a financial interest in his company as a business entity and source of income. We presume that the relevant financial amounts noted above are met.

Foreseeability

Under Regulation 18700(d) we first determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the government decision will have a financial effect on the official's financial interests. Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Ibid.)

Regulation 18701(b), provides that if the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as "a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." Further, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

In this matter, Councilmember Davis's VHR management company is not explicitly involved in the decision before the council. Accordingly, the applicable foreseeability standard is whether a financial effect "can be recognized as a realistic possibility." (Regulation 18701(b).) It does not appear to be a realistic possibility that the award of the Study contract in, and of itself, will have a financial effect on Councilmember Davis's financial interests. The city council is deciding whether to award the Study contract in response to proposals by various consultants. The decision would authorize the study of VHR impacts, which will then influence the council's VHR policy decisions. Councilmember Davis currently does not vote on items regarding the regulation of the city's VHR industry. It is not reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the award of the Study contract will have a financial effect on Councilmember Davis's interest in his VHR management company.

Because the decision before the city council is not reasonably foreseeable to have a financial effect on Councilmember Davis's financial interest, he does not have a prohibitive conflict of interest under the Act on this particular issue. (Regulation 18700(d)(1).)2

2 This conclusion only applies to the RFP and the Study, and not to contracts or decisions that may arise out of the Study

File No. A-15-222
Page No. 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By; L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

QUESTION: Does either Section 1090 or the Act limit CouncilmemberTom Davis's participation in a 
city council vote to award a contract for a socio-economic study of the impact of vacation home 
rental properties ("VHR") in the South Lake Tahoe area?

CONCLUSION: No. Given the current facts, neither law limits his participation on this particular issue. 
,
